coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional carbon dots originated from waste garlic peel for rapid sensing of heavy metals and fluorescent imaging of 2D and 3D spheroids cultured fibroblast cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Krishnaiah, Prakash
Atchudan, Raji
Perumal, Suguna
Gangadaran, Prakash
Manoj, Devaraj
Ahn, Byeong-Cheol
Kumar, Raju Suresh
Almansour, Abdulrahman I.
Lee, Yong Rok
Jeon, Byong-Hun
coredata.dc:description: Here, we prepared sulfur and nitrogen self-doped carbon dots derived from garlic peel extract (GPSNCDs) using a hydrothermal method. The as-synthesized GPSNCDs were confirmed using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and transmission electron microscopy. The analytical techniques indicate that the resulting GPSNCDs exhibit distinct emissive carbon-core with functionalities (owing to various ligands in the GPSNCDs). These functionalities are responsible for excellent hydrophilic and optical properties, including excitation-dependent emission and anti-photobleaching. Fluorescence intensities of GPSNCDs were quenched in the existence of Mn2+ and Fe3+ ions. This indicates that the GPSNCDs were sensitive to Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions with a limited range from 5 to 50 µM and showed lower recognition at ∼0.75 and 0.95 µM, respectively. In addition, the sensing results were generated in a short time (20 s). The cytotoxicity of GPSNCDs was tested to demonstrate that they are sufficiently safe to use for cellular imaging. The novel fluorescent GPSNCDs-based sensor can be used as a high-performance sensor for environmental monitoring. Further, GPSNCDs showed greater biocompatibility with normal fibroblast cells, and In Vitro fluorescent imaging of GPSNCDs revealed strong fluorescence signals in the two-dimensional (2D) and three-dimensional (3D) spheroids cultured fibroblast cells. The properties mentioned above demonstrate that the GPSNCDs can be applied to imaging normal cells without further modifications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Garlic peel
Carbon dots
Fluorescence sensor
Detection of metal ions
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Multicolor imaging of 2D cultured cells and 3D spheroids using GPSNCDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) grant funded by the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE) of the South Korean government (No.20206410100040). The Project was also supported by the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2023R142), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of as-synthesized GPSNCDs (Inset (a): size distribution graph).
(a) XRD pattern and (b) Raman spectrum of as-synthesized GPSNCDs.
XPS survey-scan spectrum (a), high-resolution XPS spectrum of carbon (b), oxygen (c), nitrogen (d), sulfur (e), and elemental composition percentage (f) of synthesized GPSNCDs.

(a) UV–vis absorbance spectrum, (b) fluorescence excitation and emission spectra, (c) excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra, and (d) normalized fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths from 280 to 430 nm of GPSNCDs.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of GPSNCDs at different irradiation times from 0 to 120 min (Inset: digital photographs of aqueous GPSNCDs at different irradiation times under 365 nm UV light) and (b) fluorescence emission spectra of GPSNCDs at different storage times from 0 to 120 days (Inset: digital photographs of aqueous GPSNCDs at different storage times).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of GPSNCDs in the presence of various metal ions (@concentration of 1000 μM) and (b) relative fluorescence intensities (F/F0) of GPSNCDs with various metal ions. (c) quenching of the fluorescence emission intensity of GPSNCDs on the addition of Fe3+ ions with different concentrations, and (d) the linear relationship between fluorescence quenching of GPSNCDs and Fe3+ ion concentration in the range of 10–50 μM. (e) quenching of the fluorescence emission intensity of GPSNCDs on the addition of various concentrations of Mn2+ ions and (f) the linear relationship between fluorescence quenching of GPSNCDs and Mn2+ ion concentration in the range of 10–50 μM.

GPSNCDs on cell viability and multicolor imaging of 2D cultured mouse normal fibroblast cells. (a) A diagram of the culture of cells in 2D cell culture plates, Created with BioRender.com. (b) The CCK8 assay results of 2D fibroblast cells were treated with various concentrations of the GPSNCDs (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, and 50 µg/mL) for 24 h. The measurements are represented in bar graph in percentages (n = 3). The student’s t-test was used for statistical analysis. *p < 0.05; **p < 0.01 and ns, not significant. (c) Laser confocal microscopy images of fibroblast cells incubated with 25 μg/mL of GPSNCDs for 24 h. Scale bar: 20 μm.

GPSNCDs on cell viability and multicolor imaging of 3D spheroid cultured mouse normal fibroblast cells. (a) A diagram of the culture of cells in 3D U-shaped cell culture plates, Created with BioRender.com. (b) The CCK8 assay results of fibroblast 3D spheroid treated with the synthesized GPSNCDs (25 µg/mL) for 24 h. The measurements are represented in bar graph form as percentages (n = 3). The student’s t-test was used for statistical analysis. ns, not significant. (c) Fluorescence microscopy images of fibroblast 3D spheroid incubated with 25 μg/mL of GPSNCDs for 24 h. Scale bar: 100 pixels.
Synthesis and applications of GPSNCDs from waste garlic peel by simple hydrothermal method. Created with BioRender.com.
Comparison of the fluorescent sensors from various carbon precursors for the selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions in a water medium.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional carbon dots originated from waste garlic peel for rapid sensing of heavy metals and fluorescent imaging of 2D and 3D spheroids cultured fibroblast cells

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors have contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we prepared sulfur and nitrogen self-doped carbon dots derived from garlic peel extract (GPSNCDs) using a hydrothermal method. The as-synthesized GPSNCDs were confirmed using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and transmission electron microscopy. The analytical techniques indicate that the resulting GPSNCDs exhibit distinct emissive carbon-core with functionalities (owing to various ligands in the GPSNCDs). These functionalities are responsible for excellent hydrophilic and optical properties, including excitation-dependent emission and anti-photobleaching. Fluorescence intensities of GPSNCDs were quenched in the existence of Mn2+ and Fe3+ ions. This indicates that the GPSNCDs were sensitive to Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions with a limited range from 5 to 50 µM and showed lower recognition at ∼0.75 and 0.95 µM, respectively. In addition, the sensing results were generated in a short time (20 s). The cytotoxicity of GPSNCDs was tested to demonstrate that they are sufficiently safe to use for cellular imaging. The novel fluorescent GPSNCDs-based sensor can be used as a high-performance sensor for environmental monitoring. Further, GPSNCDs showed greater biocompatibility with normal fibroblast cells, and In Vitro fluorescent imaging of GPSNCDs revealed strong fluorescence signals in the two-dimensional (2D) and three-dimensional (3D) spheroids cultured fibroblast cells. The properties mentioned above demonstrate that the GPSNCDs can be applied to imaging normal cells without further modifications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are emerging as zero-dimensional carbon-based materials with unique surface properties. CDs have incredibly fascinated the scientific community due to their excellent physicochemical properties such as tunable excitation-emission properties, wide-ranging excitation spectra, low-cost synthesis, less toxicity, good biocompatibility, and environmentally friendly, good hydrophilicity, and tunable surface functionalization [1–4]. The above properties of CDs make superior materials for various applications, including cell imaging, energy storage, heavy metal ion sensing and biomolecules, targeted drug delivery, solar cells, and photocatalysis [5–10].

Various naturally available plant parts have been used as carbon precursors to prepare CDs [11–14]. The CDs obtained through these eco-friendly precursors show stable fluorescence performance. However, challenges still exist, with low quantum yield (QY) and failure in target delivery, which leads to limited application in the biological system [10]. In recent times, researchers found that the above difficulties can be overcome by the heteroatoms doped CDs that alter the intrinsic properties of CDs by altering the energy gap. Hence, many types of heteroatoms, such as nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, boron, and fluorine, have been doped to CDs to improve their QY, which is essential for practical applications [15,16]. The obtained CDs showed a high QY of 36.8% and showed the ability to sense many different heavy metals including Fe3+ and Hg2+ ions in the water samples [17,18]. Compared to nitrogen-doped or sulfur-doped CDs, the co-doped CDs with nitrogen and sulfur showed a synergistic effect on the properties of CDs. This includes emission at longer wavelengths, improved QY, and showed excellent electrocatalytic and photocatalytic activities [19,20]. However, the preparation of co-doped CDs with nitrogen and sulfur (N/S-CDs) are involving extreme conditions and needed extra purification (or passivation) processes. Thus, a simple, convenient, and eco-friendly method to obtain N/S-CDs is still in the development stage [21].

Iron is one of the crucial elements in all organisms and biological systems, which plays a prominent role in various biological developments, including oxygen transportation and exchange and enzymatic reactions at the cellular level. Therefore, detecting carcinogenic transition metal ions, specifically Fe3+, is extremely important due to deepening worries about increasing environmental pollution [22]. The iron disparity has been associated with numerous ailments such as iron deficiency hemochromatosis, anemia, and Alzheimer’s disease [23]. It can be a biological inducement for synergetic fungal pathogens such as Cryptococcus neoformans and Candida albicans to convert into dangerous pathogens that cause further infections. For this, the fluorescent probes can be used to observe using a fluorescence microscope that can provide information and their responses to variations in biological stimuli [24].

Cellular imaging evaluates cellular (living/dead cells) structure and functions using imaging instruments. Fluorescent cellular imaging technology started and has constantly been evolving after discovering green fluorescent protein (GFP), even earning a Nobel Prize in Chemistry for the discovery of GFP [25,26]. Most fluorescent proteins show reduced photostable and weaker fluorescence signals because of this it is difficult for long-term imaging applications [27,28]. So, developing a new fluorescent agent with better characteristics became crucial for longer-term fluorescent cellular imaging. Fibroblasts are a common type of cell found in connective tissues throughout the body. Their abundance makes them readily to use for cell based therapies. Fibroblasts play a crucial role in wound healing and tissue repair [29]. Three-dimensional (3D) cell culture has preceded a transformation in cell culture. Cells grown in 3D can form more natural complex cell–cell interactions such as organs/tissue of their origins, which more closely mimic the in vivo situation than the two-dimensional (2D) cell culture [30].

Using a facile hydrothermal method, this investigation synthesized sulfur and nitrogen self-doped CDs (GPSNCDs) derived from garlic peel waste biomass. It is known that garlic peel (GP) contains a rich value of nitrogen and sulfur [31]. GP is a common bio-waste in all food markets and stores worldwide and generates tons of waste. The main aim of this work is focused on the possible reuse of biowaste. Using bio-waste such as GP to synthesize fluorescent CDs effectively utilizes waste into useful products. To confirm the surface functionalization and size distribution of GPSNCDs, it was subjected to various techniques, including FTIR, XRD, Raman, XPS, and TEM analysis. The synthesized GPSNCDs are used as a fluorescence probe for sensing heavy metal ions. Imaging fibroblasts can provide valuable insights into their dynamics and interactions with other cell types, helping to improve our understanding of tissue regeneration. Fibroblasts interact with the extracellular matrix, influencing tissue architecture and mechanical properties. In addition, the compatibility of GPSNCDs was analyzed in normal fibroblast cells and applied for imaging of 2D and 3D cultured cells without further modifications.

As described in our previous studies, a facile hydrothermal method was used to prepare GPSNCDs [32,33]. 0.5 g of dried GP biomass waste was grounded and added to double-distilled water (50 mL), and the solution was sonicated (30 min). After that, the dispersed solution was taken in a Teflon-coated autoclave. The autoclave was closed tightly and kept undisturbed in a conventional oven for 36 h at 180 °C (Scheme 1
). Then the obtained mixture was passed through a membrane of 0.22 μm to eliminate the aggregated particles. After filtration, the mixture was dried to obtain GPSNCDs as a solid product by freeze-drying and used for further characterization.

GPSNCDs were observed under TEM to investigate the surface structure and distribution. The TEM images are represented in Fig. 1
(a and b). As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the CDs appeared to have ring-shaped morphology and were well scattered, showing the uniform distribution of CDs across the solution. The average diameter of the GPSNCDs was 3.5 nm (Fig. 1 (a)). The HRTEM image showed a narrow size distribution between 2.0 and 5.0 nm as shown in Fig. 1 (b).

The as-synthesized GPSNCDs were characterized by XRD for phase identification and degree of crystallinity. Fig. 2
(a) represents the XRD pattern of the as-synthesized GPSNCDs displayed two broad peaks around 23.7 and 42°, that resembled graphitic lattice spacing (002) and (100), respectively. The broad peak at 23.7° is attributed to the typically moderate degree of C (002) and C(100) planes for the hexagonal graphitic structure in the GPSNCDs [34]. Raman spectrophotometer is one of the powerful techniques for characterizing carbon-based nanomaterials. The Raman spectrum of as-synthesized GPSNCDs is illustrated in Fig. 2 (b), revealing that the two different peaks at around 1355 and 1584 cm−1 correspond to D-band and G-band, respectively [35]. D-band is associated with the vibration of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in a 2D hexagonal lattice. The G-band is linked with imperfections of graphitic lattice, also responsible for the sp3-hybridized carbon networks. The degree of graphitization of GPSNCDs was investigated using the comparative intensity of two peaks (ID/IG) ratio and calculated as 0.85, which suggests the moderate degree of graphitization of GPSNCDs [36–38]. This result agrees with the XRD results. The D-band and G-band have unclear separation in the Raman spectrum of GPSNCDs, in this case, the prediction of the degree of graphitization is complicated by using the intensity of the ID/IG ratio. Hence, the deconvolution of the D and G bands was carried out, and the degree of graphitization was measured using the area of the D-band to G-band (AD/AG) ratio. The Raman spectrum of GPSNCDs with the deconvolution of the D- and G-bands is shown in Fig. S1. The measured value of the AD/AG was 0.88, which is close to the ID/IG value. This value indicates the formation of GPSNCDs had rich surface disorder/defects (moderate degree of graphitization) [39]. Surface disorder/defects are due to the existence of functional groups on the surface and edges of the GPSNCDs [14]. These results strongly demonstrated that the as-synthesized GPSNCDs had a moderate degree of graphitization with rich surface functionalities.

To investigate the functional groups and composition of the as-prepared GPSNCDs, FT-IR spectroscopy was carried out (Fig. 3 ). The peaks in the region between 3530 and 3200 cm−1 are associated with the O−H/N−H bond stretching, which is propitious to improve the hydrophilicity of the GPSNCDs. The absorption peaks 2921 and 2870 cm−1 are connected with C−H asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations, respectively. The prominent peaks at 1705, 1592, 1258, and 760 cm−1 are associated with C=O, C=C/C=N, C−O−C/C−OH, and C−S bands functional groups, respectively [34,40,41]. The shoulder peaks 1436 and 1380 cm−1 confirm the presence of C−N and C=S functionalities, confirming the presence of sulfur and nitrogen atoms in the conjugated system of carbon [42].

GPSNCDs were subjected to XPS investigation in order to determine the chemical composition. As shown in Fig. 4 , the survey scan of the XPS spectra for GPSNCDs presents four peaks at ∼167, 285, 400, and 530 eV, corresponding to sulfur (S 2p), carbon (C 1s), nitrogen (N 1s), and oxygen (O 1s), respectively [43]. The high-resolution spectra at C 1s level (Fig. 4 (b)) exhibit three distinct carbon states, 284.6, 285.7, and 288.5 eV, which are attributed to C−C/C=C, C−S/C−N/C−O, and C=O/C=N species, respectively, indicating that the carbon is present in various chemical environments [44–46]. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum ( Fig. 4 (c)) showed three peaks at 531.5, 532.8, and 535.2 eV, assigned to C=O, O=C−OH, and H−O−H groups, respectively [47]. On deconvolution of N 1s (Fig. 4 (d)) resulted in two peaks at 399.7 and 401.1 eV that correspond to the pyrrolic-N and graphite-N, respectively [48,49]. Fig. 4 (e) reveals two peaks which on deconvolution resulted in four peaks that are attributed to S p3/2 (162.7 eV), S p1/2 (164 eV), C−Sox (165.5 eV), and S=O (168.8 eV) bonds [36,50]. This reveals that major sulfur atoms present as sulfonate functional groups on the surface of the CDs. The table (Fig. 4 (f)) shows the atomic weight percentage of all four elements, including C, O, N, and S, present in the as-synthesized GPSNCDs.

The optical properties of the obtained GPSNCDs were investigated by Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) and fluorescence spectrophotometry. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the obtained aqueous GPSNCDs in Fig. 5
(a) displays broad shoulders peaks centered at ∼270 and 301 nm, that are ascribed to aromatic sp2 domains (π–π⁎ transition in C=C bonds) and random energy levels of GPSNCDs created by the surface functionalities (n−π transition in C=O bonds), respectively [51]. These results are consistent with the FT-IR and XPS results. The inset photographic images in Fig. 5 (a) reveal the pale yellow color under visible light (daylight, left) and cyan-blue color under UV light (right) at 365 nm irradiation in the dark. Generally, on UV light irradiation, the CDs with sizes nearly 5 nm will emit a cyan-blue color [52]. Fig. 5 (b) shows the maximum fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the GPSNCDs. The maximum excitation and emission wavelength were observed at about 330 and 407 nm, respectively. The fluorescence excitation-dependent emission spectra of the obtained aqueous GPSNCDs with progressively increasing wavelength by 10 nm (Fig. 5 (c)). Fig. 5 (c) reveals the sequential increase of fluorescence emission intensity up to the excitation of 330 nm.

Further increase in excitation wavelength resulted in a drop in fluorescence emission intensity. The obtained CDs showed the highest fluorescence emission intensity (brightest/strongest fluorescence) at 330 nm as an excitation wavelength. The quantum yield of obtained GPSNCDs was calculated to be ∼10%. Besides, the fluorescence emission exhibits a redshift with an increment of the excitation wavelength ranging from 280 to 430 nm. The shift is more perceptible when the fluorescence intensity is normalized at different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 5 (d)). Hence, the fluorescence spectrophotometry results conclude that the obtained GPSNCDs exhibited an excitation-dependent emission behavior due to their crystalline/graphitic domains and might be of various surface functionalities [53].

To investigate the photostability of the aqueous solution of GPSNCDs, a photobleaching experiment was carried out under nonstop irradiation of 365 nm UV light (12 W, 365 nm). As shown in Fig. 6
(a) unnoticeable changes in the fluorescence emission intensity up to 120 min of UV irradiation were observed. In addition, the pictorial fluorescence images (inset Fig. 6 (a)) of GPSNCDs solution at different time intervals reveal insignificant changes in the fluorescence. This confirms the photostability of the obtained GPSNCDs. Also, the long-term stability of the obtained aqueous GPSNCDs solution was examined by measuring the fluorescence emission (Fig. 6 (b)) at different time intervals/storage. The fluorescence emission intensities of the aqueous GPSNCDs are nearly identical at various storage times from 1st to 120th days. Also, this supports from the insignificant changes of the digital photographs of GPSNCDs solution under 365 nm UV light on the 1st and 120th day (inset Fig. 6 (b)). These insignificant changes in the fluorescence brightness confirm the photostability of GPSNCDs, which suggests practical applications.

The GPSNCDs are used as fluorescence sensors for detecting metal ions, and it is believed that the interaction between the surface/edge functional groups (–OH, –COO, and –NH) of GPSNCDs and metal ions are believed to be an interaction between the surface/edge are responsible for the sensing of metal ions. To find the selective sensing capability of GPSNCDs towards Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions in the aqueous medium, the fluorescence properties were carried out in the presence of various metal ions such as Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+ ions (1000 μM) under natural conditions. As shown in Fig. 7
(a), it is visibly seen that the GPSNCDs possess excellent selectivity for ions, Fe3+ and Mn2+. The changes of fluorescence emission intensities F/F0 (F and F0 are GPSNCDs in the presence and absence of metal ions, respectively) at the excitation of 330 nm with different metal ions (1000 μM) under the same conditions were carried out (Fig. 7 (b)). These metal ions differently influence the fluorescence behavior of GPSNCDs. The sensing influence was estimated by observing the changes in fluorescence intensity of GPSNCDs with the addition of different metal ions. Remarkably, among metal ions only Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions showed a significant and strong fluorescence quenching. A moderate quenching was observed with metal ions, Co2+ and Cu2+ ions, which might be responsible for the formation of GPSNCDs + Co2+ and GPSNCDs + Cu2+ complexes [54], respectively. However, this quenching is not as significant as that observed with Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions. Thus the synthesized GPSNCDs could be a potential applicant for an efficient and selective fluorescence sensor for Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions. The changes in the fluorescence intensities of GPSNCDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions at various concentrations was carried out to evaluate the sensing capability. As shown in Fig. 7 (c), GPSNCDs showed progressively decreased fluorescence intensity upon the addition of Fe3+ ions with a range of 10–50 μM. A good linear relationship between the (F0/F) value was obtained with R2 as 0.998 (Fig. 3 (d)). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 0.75 μM using Equation (1). The results reflect that GPSNCDs exhibit a lower detection range, which could benefit sensitive Fe3+ ion detection. Similarly, the sensing capability of the Mn2+ ions was studied (Fig. 7 (e and f)), and the LOD was calculated as 0.95 μM with a linear range of 10–50 μM (R2 = 0.997). Furthermore, we compared the reported CDs to detect Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions (Table 1 ) [32,47,54–60]. It was determined that the GPSNCDs exhibited comparable or even better performance, including wider linear range and LODs (0.75 and 0.95 μM).
(1)
LOD =
3 σ
S
Where σ is the standard deviation (n = 5) and S is the slope of the linear calibration plot.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multicolor imaging of 2D cultured cells and 3D spheroids using GPSNCDs

ce:section.ce:para: Imaging studies can elucidate how fibroblasts remodel the matrix and how these interactions impact tissue function. Fibroblasts are known to migrate within tissues during wound healing and development. Studying their migration patterns and motility behavior through imaging can provide insights into factors that regulate cell movement and tissue morphogenesis [61,62].

CCK-8 assays revealed that the newly synthesized GPSNCDs have excellent biocompatibility (Fig. 8
(a and b)). 2D cultured mouse normal fibroblast cells remained viable when cultured for 24 h at 37 °C in the presence of various concentrations of GPSNCDs. Cell viability was greater than 96.6 %, even at the GPSNCDs concentration of 40 μg/L, indicating excellent biocompatibility (Fig. 8 (a and b)). Confocal laser scanning microscope images (Fig. 8 (c)) of 2D cultured mouse normal fibroblast cells incubated with GPSNCDs for 24 h at 37 °C showed a multicolor fluorescent clear signal (blue, green, and red) inside the cells indicating the cellular uptake. GPSNCDs were localized throughout the cells (cytoplasm and nucleus), indicating that the GPSNCDs had successfully entered the mouse normal fibroblast cells. Cells without GPSNCDs showed no fluorescence signals had been found in the cells. These results agree with other studies [14,33] and GPSNCDs emit multicolor compared to other fluorescent labeling agents [63–65]. These results proved that the GPSNCDs were promising for 2D cellular imaging applications.

Advances in imaging techniques may allow for the development of 3D culture models that better mimic the in vivo environment. Fibroblasts can be incorporated into these models to study cell–cell and cell-matrix interactions in a more physiologically relevant context. 3D fibroblast culture models are particularly useful for studying wound healing and tissue repair processes. Imaging these models allows researchers to track how fibroblasts migrate, proliferate, and contribute to tissue regeneration within a more physiologically relevant environment [66].

CCK-8 assays revealed that the GPSNCDs have excellent biocompatibility (Fig. 9
(a and b)). 3D spheroid cultured mouse normal fibroblast cells remained viable when cultured for 24 h at 37 °C in the presence of increasing concentrations of GPSNCDs. 3D spheroid cell viability was greater than 99.14 % at the GPSNCDs concentration of 25 μg/L, indicating excellent biocompatibility (Fig. 9 (a and b)). Fluorescent microscope images (Fig. 9 (c)) of 3D spheroid cultured mouse normal fibroblast cells incubated with GPSNCDs for 24 h at 37 °C showed a multicolor fluorescent clear signal (blue, green, and red) inside the cells indicating the greater uptake in 3D spheroids. GPSNCDs were localized throughout the 3D spheroid (periphery and inner core), indicating that the GPSNCDs have successfully entered the 3D spheroid of mouse normal fibroblast cells. 3D spheroid without GPSNCDs showed no fluorescence signals had been found in the cells. The 3D spheroids were fluorescently imagined by transducing cells with fluorescent proteins like GFP, RFP, etc., where transduction required tedious laboratory work [67]. However, GPSNCDs were easily labeled and image 3D spheroids immediately after incubation for a specific time. In other cases, 3D spheroids were fixed, and sections were made to see the 3D spheroids by labeling single or more fluorescent labeling with antibodies [68–70]. However, our current study has performed live imaging of 3D spheroids without sectioning. These results proved that the GPSNCDs are promising fluorescent dyes for 3D spheroid imaging.

We have demonstrated a simple and green synthesis route for the synthesis of fluorescent sulfur and nitrogen self-doped CDs from GP bio-waste by a hydrothermal technique. FTIR and XPS analysis confirmed the presence of functional groups, including amine and sulfur. TEM, Raman, and XRD analysis confirmed the spherical morphology and moderate graphitic nature of the synthesized GPSNCDs. The surface/edge functional groups such as –OH, –COO, S, and –NH increased water dispersibility and optical properties of GPSNCDs. The GPSNCDs emitted a bright cyan-blue color under UV light without additional surface modification or passivation. The GPSNCDs demonstrated an acceptable quantum yield of 10% and exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence emission behavior as well as stable and intense fluorescence. The reduction in fluorescence intensity of GPSNCDs was observed in the presence of Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions. The GPSNCDs were employed as the fluorescence sensor for the selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions. Further, the detection of Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions showed good linearity from 10 to 50 μM with a lower LOD of 0.75 and 0.95 μM, respectively. The GPSNCDs possess strong binding, good selectivity, and high sensitivity toward Fe3+/Mn2+ ions. Thus, the GPSNCDs might be used as a valuable environmentally friendly nanomaterial for environmental and biosensing applications in the future. The GPSNCDs showed excellent biocompatibility with 2D cultured and 3D spheroids of mouse normal fibroblast cells. Also, they showed that extraordinary multicolor fluorescence emission of GPSNCDs will help label both 2D cultured cells and 3D spheroids. Thus, GPSNCDs might be used as a dye for imaging 2D cultured cells and 3D spheroids.

Prakash Krishnaiah: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Raji Atchudan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Suguna Perumal: Writing – review & editing. Prakash Gangadaran: Formal analysis, Data curation. Devaraj Manoj: Formal analysis, Investigation. Byeong-Cheol Ahn: Visualization, Funding acquisition. Raju Suresh Kumar: Investigation, Visualization. Abdulrahman I. Almansour: Visualization, Funding acquisition. Yong Rok Lee: Supervision. Byong-Hun Jeon: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) grant funded by the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE) of the South Korean government (No.20206410100040). The Project was also supported by the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2023R142), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123422.

ce:section.ce:para:
